Chunk # 0.
A METHOD FOR ISOLATING INTACT MITOCHONDRIA AND 
NUCLEI FROM THE SAME HOMOGENATE, AND THE IN- 
FLUENCE OF MITOCHONDRIAL DESTRUCTION ON THE 
PROPERTIES OF CELL NUCLEI* 
BY ALEXANDER L DOUNCE, PH.D., ROBERT F WITTER, PH.D., KENNETH 
J MONTY, SIDNEY PATE, AND MARY A COTTONE 
(From the Department of Biochemistry, The University of Rochester Sckool of Medicine 
and Dentistry, Rochester, New York) 
(Received for publication, November 10, 1954) 
In this paper we wish to describe what we consider to be improved tech- 
niques that permit the isolation of mitochondrla and cell nuclei from the same 
liver homogenate In the past it has been necessary to use different types of 
homogenates in isolating mltochondria and nuclei of acceptable quality We 
also wish to demonstrate the importance of maintaining mitochondrial in- 
tegrity during the isolation of nuclei (1, 2) and we offer evidence indicating 
that if the mitochondria are disrupted, an enzyme contained within them 
escapes and enters the nuclei, altering certain of their properties 
The mitochondrla produced by the new technique not only are well pre- 
served from the standpoint of morphology, but also fulfill two criteria that 
have been established for biochemically intact mitochondria (3), namely, 
that they have latent ATP-ase activity and that they require the presence of 
a phosphate acceptor for optimal oxidase 1 activity in the presence of those 
substrates, whose oxidation is coupled to phosphorylation Previously, mito- 
chondria which satisfied these two criteria have been altered morphologically 
(4, 5) In this paper a detailed comparison is made of oxidase activities of 
mitochondrla isolated by the new procedure with the corresponding activities 
of those isolated in 0.25 M sucrose which are biochemically intact but mor- 
phologically altered The ATP-ase activity of mitochondrla isolated by the 
new procedure is described in another publication (6) 
The new method has been applied mainly to rat liver, but it would probably 
work with other soft tissues, although in certain cases special steps to remove 
fiber would be necessary The method fails when applied to Walker carcinoma 
* We gratefully acknowledge the support of the National Cancer Institute (Grant C-994) 
and the National Heart Institute (Grant H-1616) of the National Institutes of Health, 
United States Public Health Service 
l By the term oxidase we are referring to sueeinoxidase, a-ketoglutarate, malate, and 
hexanoate oxidase

256, as have other methods involving the use of aqueous solvent, but we have 
developed an alternative procedure which can be applied both to liver and 
to the above-mentioned tumor This latter method does not however provide 
unaltered mitochondria 
The mitochondrial enzyme referred to above, which affects certain properties 
of the nuclei, appears to detach desoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) from some 
protein of the nucleus to which, we maintain, it is otherwise firmly attached 
Nuclei that have not been subjected to the action of this enzyme are capable 
of forming gels in dilute alkali or strong sodium chloride (2, 7, 8), whereas 
after action of this mitochondrial enzyme, no such gels can be formed Gel 
formation is considered to be an indication that the DNA is still firmly bound 
to nuclear protein, evidently by bonds other than those which are electrostatic 
in nature This general concept has been advanced by us previously (8), but 
apparently has not been widely accepted We believe that our new work with 
the action of the mitochondrial enzyme constitutes further evidence in its 
favor In an accompanying paper (27) we have summarized the various means 
which can be utilized to prevent action of the mitochondrial enzyme on the 
nuclei 
The work described in this paper is to a considerable extent based upon 
the use of a new type of glass homogenizer (1, 2) which in our opinion offers 
certain definite advantages over homogenizers previously available for work 
with soft tissues The details of the construction and use of this new homog- 
enizer are presented in the section of this paper on experimental procedures 
Also the ATP-ase activities * of mitochondria prepared with the new homog- 
enizer are compared with those of mitochondria isolated with the aid of an old 
style homogenizer 
Methods 
Descriplion and Construction of the New Homogenlzer.--Fig 1 shows a photograph of 
the new homogenizers A description can be found in Vol 2 of The Nucleic Acids (2) The 
clearance of the "loosely fitting plunger" is about 0.001 to 0.0015 inch, at the circumference 
of the ball, and the clearance of the "tightly fitting plunger" is in the neighborhood of 0.0005 
inch

mitochondria.protocol:
**Steps:**

1. The authors describe improved techniques for isolating mitochondria and cell nuclei from the same liver homogenate.
2. In the past, different types of homogenates were used to isolate mitochondria and nuclei of acceptable quality.
3. The authors aim to demonstrate the importance of maintaining mitochondrial integrity during the isolation of nuclei.
4. If mitochondria are disrupted, an enzyme contained within them escapes and enters the nuclei, altering certain properties of the nuclei.
5. The mitochondria produced by the new technique are well preserved in terms of morphology.
6. The new method produces mitochondria that fulfill two criteria for biochemically intact mitochondria: latent ATP-ase activity and the requirement of a phosphate acceptor for optimal oxidase activity.
7. A detailed comparison is made between the oxidase activities of mitochondria isolated by the new procedure and those isolated in 0.25 M sucrose, which are biochemically intact but morphologically altered.
8. The ATP-ase activity of mitochondria isolated by the new procedure is described in another publication.
9. The new method has been mainly applied to rat liver, but it may work with other soft tissues, requiring special steps to remove fiber in certain cases.
10. The method fails when applied to Walker carcinoma.
11. The authors have developed an alternative procedure that can be applied to both liver and Walker carcinoma, but it does not provide unaltered mitochondria.
12. The mitochondrial enzyme mentioned earlier affects certain properties of the nuclei.
13. The enzyme appears to detach DNA from a protein of the nucleus to which it is otherwise firmly attached.
14. Nuclei that have not been subjected to the action of this enzyme are capable of forming gels in dilute alkali or strong sodium chloride, but after the action of the mitochondrial enzyme, no such gels can be formed.
15. Gel formation indicates that DNA is still firmly bound to nuclear protein by bonds other than those which are electrostatic in nature.
16. The authors believe that their new work with the mitochondrial enzyme provides further evidence in favor of the concept that DNA is firmly bound to nuclear protein.
17. In an accompanying paper, the authors summarize various means to prevent the action of the mitochondrial enzyme on the nuclei.
18. The work described in the paper is based on the use of a new type of glass homogenizer.
19. The new homogenizer offers certain definite advantages over previously available homogenizers for work with soft tissues.
20. The details of the construction and use of the new homogenizer are presented in the section of the paper on experimental procedures.
21. The ATP-ase activities of mitochondria prepared with the new homogenizer are compared with those of mitochondria isolated with an old style homogenizer.

**Conclusion:**
The article describes improved techniques for isolating mitochondria and cell nuclei from the same liver homogenate. The authors emphasize the importance of maintaining mitochondrial integrity during the isolation of nuclei and provide evidence that disruption of mitochondria leads to the release of an enzyme that alters certain properties of the nuclei. The article mentions the criteria for biochemically intact mitochondria and compares the activities of mitochondria isolated using the new technique with those isolated using a different method. The article also discusses the use of a new type of glass homogenizer and presents a comparison of ATP-ase activities of mitochondria prepared with the new homogenizer and an old style homogenizer. However, the article does not provide a detailed protocol for the isolation of mitochondria and nuclei, and some steps are mentioned without specific details.

summary:
Revised Summary (Iteration 2):
Researchers Alexander L. Dounce, Robert F. Witter, Kenneth J. Monty, Sidney Pate, and Mary A. Cottone collaborated with various institutions to develop improved techniques for isolating intact mitochondria and nuclei from liver homogenates. They used a new glass homogenizer and special steps to maintain mitochondrial integrity. The study emphasized the importance of biochemically intact mitochondria and compared their ATP-ase activity to morphologically altered mitochondria. Additionally, they investigated the detachment of DNA from nuclear proteins and the formation of gels as indicators of cellular processes. The findings provided insights into isolation techniques and the role of mitochondrial enzymes in altering nuclear properties. The researchers also introduced a new design of glass homogenizer for improved isolation procedures, and compared ATP-ase activities in different experimental procedures.

mitochondria.protocol:
Isolation of Mitochondria and Nuclei from Rat Liver in 0.44 M Sucrose Adjusted to pH 6.2 with Citric Acid:

1. "In the following procedure great care must be taken to keep the preparation as cold as possible without freezing, especially if mitochondria are to be isolated."
2. "All equipment such as mortars, pestles, homogenizers, homogenizer pestles, and centrifuge cups must be thoroughly chilled at 0 to 2 °C before use."
3. "It should be noted that the tissue or any surface upon which the tissue is placed should not be wet if mitochondria are to be isolated, although in the isolation of nuclei only, the addition of a few small pieces of cracked ice during the filtrations through cheese-cloth does no harm."
4. "All solutions should be kept at 0 to 2 °C."
5. "For isolation of mitochondria, centrifugation is carried out in the 50 ml cups of the International centrifuge and in the 25 ml cups of the high-speed head attachment."
6. "For simultaneous preparation of nuclei and mitochondria, it is necessary to have available two refrigerated centrifuges."

Preparation of Homogenate:

1. "25 g of liver are quickly excised from decapitated rats."
2. "This tissue is immediately minced in a chilled Petri dish which is kept in an ice bath."
3. "After the weighed liver has been gently ground up with a pestle in a chilled mortar which is kept in an ice bath, 7 g portions of the tissue pulp are transferred to a chilled homogenizer tube immersed in an ice bath."
4. "28 ml of 0.44 M (15 percent) sucrose solution containing 0.63 ml of 0.1 M citric acid is added."
5. "The liver is quickly homogenized, with 8 passes of the chilled 'loosely fitting' plunger."
6. "The pH of an aliquot (0.5 to 1.0 ml) which has been allowed to warm up to room temperature is measured with the Beckman pH meter, and this small aliquot is then discarded."
7. "The pH should not be lower than 6.0 or higher than 6.2."
8. "In working with nuclei, pH values close to 6.0 are the safest."
9. "If the pH is too low, the homogenate must be discarded."
10. "On the other hand, if the pH is too high, a few drops of 0.01 M citric acid in 0.44 M sucrose are added until the proper value is attained."
11. "Then the homogenate is transferred to a chilled graduate which is kept in an ice bath."
12. "The homogenization and adjustment of pH are repeated with the second and third batches of liver."

**Conclusion:**
The protocol for the isolation of mitochondria and nuclei from rat liver in 0.44 M sucrose adjusted to pH 6.2 with citric acid is described in the article. The steps for the preparation of the homogenate are provided in detail, including the use of chilled equipment, the addition of sucrose solution and citric acid, homogenization with a plunger, and pH adjustment. However, there are some gaps in the protocol, such as the specific duration or speed of homogenization and the temperature and duration of centrifugation. These missing details may be crucial for accurately replicating the protocol.

The pellets are resuspended with the aid of a stirring rod in a total of 40 ml of 0.44 M sucrose

the homogenate described above under A is filtered successively through four layers of Nos 
60, 90, and 120 cheese-cloth (Curity, Kendall Mills Inc., Walpole, Massachusetts) It is not 
a good plan to squeeze out the cheese-cloth filters, and if the filtration time is to be kept short, 
it may be necessary to accept a considerable loss of homogenate For best results, the filtration 
should be carried out in a cold room, but, in lieu of this, the temperature can be kept down by 
adding a small amount of cracked ice to the material before filtration provided tile mitochon-

The next step is to effect a more complete homogenization than is necessary if only mito-

mitochondria.protocol:
Isolation of the Mitochondrial Fraction:
1. Dilute one-fifth (25 ml) of the combined and mixed homogenate with enough chilled 0.44 M sucrose to bring the final volume to 50 ml.
2. Centrifuge the diluted homogenate at 675 g for 20 minutes.
3. Decant the supernatant into chilled graduates immersed in an ice bath.
4. Disperse the precipitate in 30 ml of 0.44 M sucrose by 3 or 4 passes of the chilled "loosely fitting" plunger.
5. Centrifuge the suspension at 675 g for 20 minutes.
6. Decant the supernatant and discard the nuclear precipitate.
7. Centrifuge the combined supernatants from the low-speed centrifugations at 13,000 g for 10 minutes.
8. Decant the supernatants and use them for the preparation of the "microsome" and "soluble" phases if desired.
9. Resuspend the pellets in a total of 40 ml of 0.44 M sucrose with the aid of a stirring rod.
10. Centrifuge the suspension at 13,000 g for 7 minutes.
11. Wash the pellets with a total of 40 ml of 0.44 M sucrose.
12. Resuspend the resulting pellets in a total volume of 10 ml of 0.44 M sucrose using a new homogenizer.
13. Repeat the procedure with the other portions of the original homogenate if desired.

Isolation of Nuclei:
1. Filter the remaining four-fifths (100 ml) of the homogenate through four layers of cheese-cloth.
2. Homogenize the filtered homogenate in a ground glass homogenizer with a tightly fitting pestle for 12 to 24 passes.
3. Dilute the filtered homogenate by adding an equal volume of 0.44 M sucrose and thoroughly mix them.
4. Centrifuge the diluted homogenate at 380 g for 10 minutes.
5. Decant the supernatant fluid and discard it after microscopic examination to ensure low concentration of nuclei.
6. Suspend the crude nuclei sediment in 50 ml of 0.44 M sucrose by homogenization with 6 passes of a loosely fitting pestle.
7. Adjust the pH to 6.0-6.1 by adding 0.1 to 0.5 ml of 0.01 M citric acid as needed.
8. Centrifuge the suspension at 280 g for 10 minutes.
9. Discard the supernatant fluid and resuspend the nuclei in 50 ml of 0.44 M sucrose.
10. Homogenize the nuclei suspension as in the preceding step and adjust the pH to 6.0-6.1.

summary:
Revised Summary: Isolation of Mitochondria and Nuclei from Liver Tissue:
- Homogenize the liver tissue using a ground glass homogenizer.
- Dilute the homogenate with chilled 0.44 M sucrose.
- Centrifuge the diluted homogenate at 675 g for 20 minutes to separate the supernatant and the nuclear precipitate.
- Discard the nuclear precipitate and decant the supernatant.
- Wash the resulting pellets with 0.44 M sucrose and resuspend them in the same solution.
- Centrifuge the resuspended pellets at 13,000 g to obtain the mitochondrial fraction.
- Discard the supernatant and resuspend the pellets in 0.44 M sucrose.
- Filter the remaining four-fifths of the homogenate through cheese-cloth.
- Dilute the filtered homogenate with 0.44 M sucrose and centrifuge it at 380 g for 10 minutes.
- Discard the supernatant and suspend the crude nuclei sediment in 0.44 M sucrose.
- Adjust the pH to 6.0-6.1 using citric acid (0.01 M) and centrifuge at 280 g for 10 minutes.
- Discard the supernatant and resuspend the nuclei in 0.44 M sucrose.
- Adjust the pH to 6.0-6.1 and centrifuge at 195 g for 10 minutes.
- The resulting sediment is largely free from mitochondria but may still contain erythrocytes.
- The overall isolation procedure involves homogenization, dilution, centrifugation, sedimentation, and filtration.

If the mitochondria 
must be completely removed without being ruptured, as is necessary in some types of work, 
a third washing is necessary, using 50 ml of 0.44 M sucrose, and adjusting the pH if necessary 
as described previously In this washing the speed of centrifugation is reduced to 100 g (850 
K.P.M.) 
All the above steps can be carried out fairly successfully in one 250 ml centrifuge tube, 
using approximately the same centrifugation speeds as above In any case the sediments and 
supernatant fluids should always be checked until the best centrifugation schedule for the 
centrifuge at hand is finally determined 
The nuclei obtained by the procedures thus far described will be heavily contaminated 
with erythrocytes unless the livers have been perfused with 0.9 per cent sodium chloride 
and sucrose as described by Hogeboom and Schneider (9) The erythrocytes can be rather 
easily removed however by washing the nuclei twice in a 15 ml conical centrifuge tube in 1 
per cent gum arabic solution previously adjusted to pH 6.0 with NaOH, the tube being nearly 
filled with gum solution each time The centrifugations are at 125 g (1000 R.P.M.) and 90 g 
(800 R.p.xq.) respectively The erythrocytes are laked by this procedure and the stroma remains 
in the supernatant fluid 
If dry weights of the nuclei are to be determined, the nuclei must then be washed two or 
three times in water to remove the gum, but this process is not time-consuming, since 5 
minutes per spin suffices to bring down all the nuclei if a speed corresponding to 280 g to 513 
g (1500 to 2000 a.P.~.) is used 
Additional Notes on Isolation of Nucld and Mitochondrla.--In the procedures just described, 
the mitochondria and nuclei are isolated from a separate aliquot of the same homogenate 
If only mitochondria are to be prepared, 5 to 7 gin of liver are treated as described in A and 
B This amount usually provides enough mitochondria for one experiment, and a larger 
amount of tissue merely increases the time and labor involved in the preparation If only 
nuclei are to be prepared, a homogenate can be made by adding 20 gin of the pulped liver to 
80 ml of 0.44 ~t sucrose containing 1.8 ml of 0.1 x citric acid, homogenizing this in three 
portions as described in A, and then treating as described in C 
Instead of using separate aliquots of liver, mitochondria may be isolated from the first 
supernataut of the nuclear precipitate mentioned in C by the procedure described in B This 
yields a large quantity of mitochondria, which, however, may not be as suitable for studies 
of oxidative phosphorylation as those obtained from separate aliquots of homogenates, since the 
extra handling required for the isolation of nuclei may cause some loss in activity of the en- 
zymes for oxidative phosphorylation However if more stable mitochondrial enzymes are to 
be studied, the supernatant from the first nuclear sediment can be used The mitochondria 
studied in this paper were prepared from separate aliquots of liver 
It should be stated that the Waring blendor or colloid mill cannot be used for homogeni- 
zation using 0.44 ~t sucrose adjusted to pH 6.0 with citric acid, if nuclei are to be isolated The

Steps for Mitochondrial Isolation:

1. "If the mitochondria must be completely removed without being ruptured, as is necessary in some types of work, a third washing is necessary, using 50 ml of 0.44 M sucrose, and adjusting the pH if necessary as described previously. In this washing, the speed of centrifugation is reduced to 100 g (850 K.P.M.)" (source)
2. "All the above steps can be carried out fairly successfully in one 250 ml centrifuge tube, using approximately the same centrifugation speeds as above. In any case, the sediments and supernatant fluids should always be checked until the best centrifugation schedule for the centrifuge at hand is finally determined" (source)
3. "The nuclei obtained by the procedures thus far described will be heavily contaminated with erythrocytes unless the livers have been perfused with 0.9 per cent sodium chloride and sucrose as described by Hogeboom and Schneider. The erythrocytes can be rather easily removed, however, by washing the nuclei twice in a 15 ml conical centrifuge tube in 1 per cent gum arabic solution previously adjusted to pH 6.0 with NaOH, the tube being nearly filled with gum solution each time. The centrifugations are at 125 g (1000 R.P.M.) and 90 g (800 R.p.xq.) respectively. The erythrocytes are laked by this procedure and the stroma remains in the supernatant fluid" (source)
4. "If dry weights of the nuclei are to be determined, the nuclei must then be washed two or three times in water to remove the gum, but this process is not time-consuming, since 5 minutes per spin suffices to bring down all the nuclei if a speed corresponding to 280 g to 513 g (1500 to 2000 a.P.~.) is used" (source)
5. "Additional Notes on Isolation of Nuclei and Mitochondria. In the procedures just described, the mitochondria and nuclei are isolated from a separate aliquot of the same homogenate" (source)
6. "If only mitochondria are to be prepared, 5 to 7 gin of liver are treated as described in A and B. This amount usually provides enough mitochondria for one experiment, and a larger amount of tissue merely increases the time and labor involved in the preparation. If only nuclei are to be prepared, a homogenate can be made by adding 20 gin of the pulped liver to 80 ml of 0.44 ~t sucrose containing 1.8 ml of 0.1 x citric acid, homogenizing this in three portions as described in A, and then treating as described in C" (source)
7. "Instead of using separate aliquots of liver, mitochondria may be isolated from the first supernatant of the nuclear precipitate mentioned in C by the procedure described in B. This yields a large quantity of mitochondria, which, however, may not be as suitable for studies of oxidative phosphorylation as those obtained from separate aliquots of homogenates, since the extra handling required for the isolation of nuclei may cause some loss in activity of the enzymes for oxidative phosphorylation. However, if more stable mitochondrial enzymes are to be studied, the supernatant from the first nuclear sediment can be used. The mitochondria studied in this paper were prepared from separate aliquots of liver" (source)

Conclusion:
The article provides a detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation, including steps for washing with sucrose, removing erythrocytes, and washing the nuclei. However, there are some gaps in the protocol, such as the lack of specific details on the initial steps of homogenization and the absence of information on the final steps of mitochondrial isolation. mitochondria.protocol:
1. To isolate nuclei from Walker carcinoma 256, a procedure is used that involves the use of calcium chloride in the first homogenization without pH adjustment.
2. Since the Walker tumor contains a lot of fiber, a tissue press is used to remove the majority of the fiber before homogenization is possible.
3. 20 g of the pulped tissue from the tissue press is mixed with 80 mL of 0.44 M sucrose containing calcium chloride in a concentration of 0.005 g. The suspension is homogenized and filtered through cheese-cloth in three batches.
4. It may be necessary to use as many as three dozen passes of the tightly fitting plunger of the homogenizer to ensure satisfactory disruption of cells.
5. The homogenate is then diluted with an equal volume of 0.44 M sucrose containing 0.005 M CaCl2.
6. The nuclei are centrifuged down and then resuspended in about 100 mL of 0.44 M sucrose without CaCl2. The pH is adjusted to 6.0-6.1 with dilute citric acid.
7. The isolation is carried out in the same manner as described for liver cell nuclei, but washings in gum arabic are unnecessary due to the low presence of erythrocytes.
8. The isolated nuclei are free from mitochondria, but there is always a small number of whole cells present, estimated to be a contamination of 5 to 15%.
9. Another method for isolating cell nuclei from soft tissues using a ball type homogenizer and 0.25 M sucrose containing calcium chloride in a concentration of 0.005 M is mentioned but not described in detail.
10. The use of 0.44 M sucrose is found to be preferable to 0.25 M sucrose when using the ball type homogenizer, as the mitochondria do not tend to swell and the cells are more easily broken.
11. The concentration of 0.44 M sucrose is considered optimal for achieving maximal cell breakdown in the case of the Walker carcinoma.
12. Mitochondria can be isolated from the first supernatant fluid remaining after centrifugation of the nuclei in the sucrose-calcium chloride solution method. The mitochondria should be resuspended in plain 0.44 M sucrose without calcium chloride, and the pH is adjusted to 6.0-6.2. The isolation is then completed as described previously.
13. The properties of the mitochondria isolated using this method have not been thoroughly investigated, but their microscopic appearance is not as good as those isolated from liver using the first described method.
14. Mitochondria can also be isolated from 0.25 M sucrose and 0.44 M sucrose at pH 6.2 using the new homogenizer. The old motor-driven homogenizer can also be used, and it gives comparable results.
15. ATP-ase activity and fatty acid oxidase activity of the mitochondria isolated using different methods are mentioned, but no further details are provided.
16. The production of nuclear gels is mentioned, but the method for producing them is not described in detail.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for isolating nuclei from Walker carcinoma 256 using calcium chloride in the first homogenization step. However, the protocol for isolating mitochondria is not fully described, with only some steps mentioned. There is also a lack of information on the properties of the mitochondria isolated using different methods. The article mentions the production of nuclear gels but does not provide a detailed method for their production.

summary:
Revised Summary:
Researchers have developed a method to isolate nuclei from the Walker carcinoma 256 tumor using calcium chloride, a tissue press, sucrose, and a homogenizer. The process involves homogenization, filtration, and dilution of the tissue suspension with sucrose containing calcium chloride. The isolated nuclei are then resuspended in sucrose without calcium chloride. Mitochondria can also be isolated from liver using this method. The study discusses the properties of the isolated mitochondria, including ATP-ase activity and low fatty acid oxidase activity. Additionally, the researchers found that gel formation occurs when adjusting the pH of the nuclei suspension to a value of 9 to 10 with NH4OH or NaOH, but salt concentrations around 0.9% interfere with gel formation. The optimal concentration for achieving maximal cell breakdown is around 0.44 M sucrose.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Protocol:**

1. Isolation of Nuclei:
   - Two types of nuclei were used: those isolated at pH 6.0 in 0.44 M sucrose solution using a ball type homogenizer, and those isolated at pH 4.0 in distilled water using a Waring blendor for homogenization.
   - Both types of nuclei form strong gels in the presence of dilute alkali.
   - An extract of mitochondria capable of preventing gel formation in nuclei was prepared.

2. Preparation of Mitochondrial Extract:
   - Mitochondria isolated from normal rat liver in 0.44 M sucrose without pH adjustment using a ball type homogenizer were taken up in distilled water at pH 6.5-7.0 and room temperature.
   - The suspension contained the total quantity of isolated mitochondria corresponding to 0.5 gm of liver (wet weight) in a volume of 1 ml.
   - The lysed suspension was subjected to high-speed centrifugation.
   - The clear supernatant fluid was used as the source of the "degelling" enzyme.
   - A portion of this fluid was treated at 100°C for 10 minutes to obtain a control solution containing heat-denatured enzyme.

3. Gel Formation Assay:
   - Suspensions of "gellable" nuclei previously adjusted to pH 6.5-7.0 (120 to 30 mg/ml, dry weight) were prepared.
   - To each sample, 0.2 ml aliquots of the mitochondrial extract and 0.2 ml of distilled water were added.
   - The control samples contained nuclei and 0.2 ml aliquots of the heat-treated enzyme solution.
   - Total volumes were adjusted to 0.6 ml by adding 0.2 ml portions of distilled water.
   - Test and control mixtures were incubated at room temperature (22°C) for varying time intervals (15 minutes to 1.5 hours).
   - A drop of dilute NH4OH was added to each tube to induce gel formation.
   - Nuclei treated with the unheated mitochondrial extract lost their ability to form gels within the maximum incubation time, usually within 30 minutes.
   - The control samples retained their ability to form gels.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for isolating mitochondria and testing their effect on gel formation in nuclei. The steps for isolating nuclei and preparing the mitochondrial extract are clearly described. However, the article does not provide information on the specific centrifugation conditions or the exact composition of the mitochondrial extract. Additionally, the gel formation assay lacks specific details on the incubation time intervals and the concentration of dilute NH4OH used. These gaps in the protocol should be addressed to ensure reproducibility.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. The new homogenizer is superior to previously existing types for isolating cell nuclei in aqueous media.
2. Mitochondria isolated at pH 6 in 0.44 M sucrose solution compare favorably with those fixed in situ and appear to be much better preserved than mitochondria isolated by other procedures.
3. The DNA content of nuclei isolated from liver using the first method described is about 11% as determined by the Schneider technique.
4. The DNA content of nuclei isolated from liver using the alternate method involving sucrose-calcium chloride solution in the homogenization is about 14%.
5. Nuclei of Walker carcinoma 256 isolated by the alternate method using sucrose-calcium chloride for the homogenization contain about 14% DNA.
6. The new homogenizer is more effective and gentle in isolating mitochondria compared to an older type of homogenizer in 0.25 M sucrose without pH adjustment.
7. Mitochondria isolated with the new homogenizer in 0.25 M sucrose are more sensitive to damage than those isolated by the new method described in this paper.
8. ATP-ase activity of mitochondria prepared with either the new or old homogenizer in 0.25 M sucrose is very low but is increased by aging for 20 minutes at 37 °C or the presence of 1 X 10^-4 M DNP (dinitrophenol).
9. Hexanoate, malate, and a-ketoglutarate oxidases of mitochondria prepared with the new homogenizer are similar to those prepared with the old homogenizer.
10. Both types of mitochondrial suspension require the addition of a phosphate acceptor for maximum rate of oxidase activity.

**Incompleteness/Gaps:**
- The article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation.
- The specific steps for mitochondrial isolation are not clearly described.
- The article does not mention the specific buffers, centrifugation speeds, or other details necessary for a complete protocol.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides some information on mitochondrial isolation, including comparisons between different homogenizers and the preservation of mitochondria under certain conditions. However, the article lacks a complete and detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation, making it difficult to replicate the procedure accurately. Further information is needed to fill in the gaps and provide a comprehensive protocol.

Liver nuclei isolated with the new homogenizer contain fewer whole cells compared to previous methods, including those from Walker carcinoma 256. Mitochondria isolated using the new homogenizer at pH 6 in 0.44 M sucrose solution appear to be better preserved than those isolated by other procedures, as shown in Fig 2 of a previous publication. The DNA content of liver nuclei isolated using different methods varies, with higher percentages of DNA in nuclei isolated with sucrose-calcium chloride solution.

Summary: The new homogenizer, using citric acid and sucrose solution, is superior to previous methods for isolating cell nuclei in an aqueous medium. Liver nuclei isolated with the new homogenizer contain fewer whole cells compared to previous methods, including those from Walker carcinoma 256. Mitochondria isolated using the new homogenizer at pH 6 in 0.44 M sucrose solution appear to be better preserved than those isolated by other procedures, as shown in Fig 2 of a previous publication. The DNA content of liver nuclei isolated using different methods varies, with higher percentages of DNA in nuclei isolated with sucrose-calcium chloride solution. The ATP-ase and oxidase activities of mitochondria prepared with the new homogenizer are low but can be increased by aging or the presence of dinitrophenol (DNP).

article:
Chunk # 7.
A muscle fraction (16) containing the phosphocreatine transphos- 
phorylase and creatine was added in the experiments given in Table II The 
results given in Tables I and II show that the ATP-ase and oxidative enzymes 
of the mitochondria made with the new homogenizer are as good as those 
of the organdies made with the old homogenizer and compare favorably with 
those given in the literature (5, 17-19) As far as the enzymatic activity of the 
mitochondria is concerned, the new homogenizer has no advantage over the 
old one The former is, however, much more convenient to use and results 
in a more complete homogenization 
Enzymatic Activity of Mitockondria Prepared at pH 6.2.~In a previous 
paper (6) it is shown that the ATP-ase of these preparations is latent Also, 
as is illustrated in Table II, the oxidase ~ activity compares favorably to that of 
mitochondria isolated in 0.25 M sucrose which are morphologically altered but 
which have excellent oxidative activity In addition the mitochondria isolated 
in 0.44 ~ sucrose at pH 6.2, like those from 0.25 x~ sucrose, require the addition 
of a phosphate acceptor for maximum oxidase activity 
DISCUSSION 
This new method for the preparation of nuclei and mitochondria at pH 
6.2 is, as far as the authors are aware, the first procedure that can be applied 
for the isolation of clean suspensions of nuclei and mitochondrla from the 
same homogenate It is of particular significance that the mitochondria thus 
isolated not only appear to be superior in morphology to mitochondria ob- 
tainable by any of the previous methods (4-7, 18, 20) but also compare favor- 
ably in ATP-ase and oxidase activity to previous preparations of mitochondria 
which were excellent from the standpoint of enzymology but poor from the 
standpoint of morphology (4-6, 17, 18) Thus the new method should prove 
to be of value in cytochemical research 
One of the most important features of the new method of isolation is the 
maintenance of the correct pH during the homogenization by the addition 
of the exact amount of citric acid prior to homogenization At pH values 
from 5.7-4.0 agglutination occurs while at pH values higher than 6.2 some 
nuclei are destroyed It is obvious that the ratio of the amount of sucrose 
and citric acid to the amount of tissue must be determined by preliminary 
experiments and that this ratio must be kept constant in subsequent prepara- 
tions The authors have been able to obtain very consistent results by the 
procedure described, but it will be necessary for the individual investigator 
to determine the exact ratio to use under the conditions of his experiments 
and with the tissue that he wishes to study 
For the Walker carcinoma 256 the method involving sucrose-calcium 
chloride solution in the initial homogenization can be used for the isolation 
of nuclei of fair quality, but the mitochondria so produced do not appear to 
Downloaded from http://rupress.org/jcb/article-pdf/1/2/139/1456217/139.pdf by guest on 15 February 2024
DOUNCE~ WITTER, MONTY, PATE~ AND COTTONE 151 
be normal in appearance under the microscope When this method is applied 
to liver, the nuclei are of acceptable quality However, the mitochondria thus 
produced have a high initial ATP-ase (6) and virtually no fatty acid oxidase 
(Table II) 
A precaution which must always be observed with the citrate method is to 
discard the small portions of homogenates used to measure the pH, since 
for accurate results these samples of homogenates must be allowed to warm 
up to room temperature before being placed in the Beckman pH meter If 
this precaution is not observed, the nuclei may agglutinate and the ATP-ase 
activity of the mitochondrial fraction will be high 
It has been found that no improvement in the enzymatic activity or mor- 
phology of the mitochondria was obtained if the pH was kept at 6.0-6.2 
through all stages of the preparation For mitochondria, adjustment of the 
pH with citric acid appears to be necessary only at the time of homogeniza- 
tion This may be a reflection of the fact that the pH of suspensions of mito- 
chondria prepared by a variety of methods begins to fall within the range 
6.2-6.6 as the mitochondria are washed free of other components of the cell 
The exact mechanism of the protective action of citrate is unknown Citrate 
ion has been used in the isolation of nuclei but has not heretofore been em- 
ployed for mitochondrial preparations If versene is used at pH 6.2, the mito- 
chondria are abnormal in appearance Unpublished work of one of us 
(Witter) shows that citrate may protect mitochondria from swelling in hypo- 
tonic solution in much the same manner as versene or ATP (21) It remains 
to be proven whether this effect of citrate is related to the superior preserva- 
tion of morphological structure

mitochondria.protocol:
Based on the given text, it is difficult to extract the complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. However, we can identify some relevant information and note the incompleteness of the protocol.

**Extracted Steps:**
1. Prepare a muscle fraction containing phosphocreatine transphosphorylase and creatine.
2. Use a homogenizer (new or old) to make mitochondria.
3. Compare the enzymatic activity of mitochondria made with the new homogenizer to those made with the old homogenizer and literature references.
4. Note that the new homogenizer is more convenient to use and results in more complete homogenization.
5. Prepare mitochondria at pH 6.2.
6. Isolate mitochondria in 0.25 M sucrose, which are morphologically altered but have excellent oxidative activity.
7. Add a phosphate acceptor to mitochondria isolated in 0.44 M sucrose at pH 6.2 for maximum oxidase activity.
8. Use the new method for the preparation of nuclei and mitochondria at pH 6.2, which is the first procedure to isolate clean suspensions of nuclei and mitochondria from the same homogenate.
9. Note that the mitochondria isolated using the new method have superior morphology compared to previous methods.
10. Compare the ATP-ase and oxidase activity of the mitochondria isolated using the new method to previous preparations.
11. Maintain the correct pH during homogenization by adding the exact amount of citric acid prior to homogenization.
12. Determine the ratio of sucrose and citric acid to tissue amount through preliminary experiments and keep it constant in subsequent preparations.
13. Use the sucrose-calcium chloride solution method for the isolation of nuclei of fair quality in the Walker carcinoma 256.
14. Discard small portions of homogenates used to measure pH to avoid agglutination of nuclei and high ATP-ase activity in the mitochondrial fraction.
15. Adjust the pH with citric acid only at the time of homogenization for mitochondria.
16. Note that the pH of mitochondrial suspensions prepared by various methods falls within the range of 6.2-6.6 as the mitochondria are washed free of other cell components.
17. The exact mechanism of the protective action of citrate on mitochondria is unknown.

**Incompleteness:**
- The exact steps for muscle fraction preparation and homogenization are not provided.
- The specific details of the new method for the preparation of nuclei and mitochondria at pH 6.2 are not mentioned.
- The specific steps for isolating mitochondria in 0.25 M sucrose and 0.44 M sucrose at pH 6.2 are not described.
- The exact ratio of sucrose and citric acid to tissue amount is not provided.

**Conclusion:**
The extracted steps provide some information about mitochondrial isolation, including the use of a homogenizer, the importance of pH control, and the comparison of enzymatic activity. However, the protocol is incomplete, lacking specific details for muscle fraction preparation, homogenization, and sucrose-based isolation methods. Further information is needed to fully understand the mitochondrial isolation protocol described in the article.

summary: Summary 2: A muscle fraction containing phosphocreatine transphosphorylase and creatine is used in experiments with a new homogenizer. The results show that the ATP-ase and oxidative enzyme activity of the mitochondria made with the new homogenizer are comparable to those made with the old homogenizer and literature references. The enzymatic activity of the mitochondria is latent, but the oxidase activity is comparable to mitochondria isolated in 0.25 M sucrose. The new method for preparing nuclei and mitochondria at pH 6.2 yields superior morphology and ATP-ase and oxidase activity. The correct pH during homogenization is maintained by adding the exact amount of citric acid. Agglutination occurs at pH 5.7-4.0, and some nuclei are destroyed at pH >6.2. The ratio of sucrose and citric acid to tissue must be determined by preliminary experiments. Sucrose-calcium chloride solution can be used for isolating nuclei of fair quality. Precautions must be taken to discard small portions of homogenates used for pH measurement. The enzymatic activity and morphology of mitochondria are not improved if the pH is kept at 6.0-6.2 throughout the preparation. The pH of mitochondria suspensions falls within the 6.2-6.6 range after washing. The mechanism of citrate's protective action is unknown, but it may protect mitochondria from swelling in hypotonic solution. The effect of citrate on the preservation of morphological structure remains unproven.

That this cannot indeed be the whole story 
is shown by the fact that mitochondria prepared in 0.25 M sucrose adjusted to 
pH 6.2 with citrate are partially swollen 
The nature of the mitochondrial enzyme which acts upon cell nuclei in 
such a manner as to prevent subsequent gel formation in dilute alkali is not 
yet clear Any enzyme that would degrade either nucleic acid or protein might 
be the one in question, but it is also possible that a more specific enzyme is 
involved which attacks whatever linkages bind DNA to protein of the nucleus 
Although mitochondria are known to contain DNA-ase (22-24) it does not 
seem very likely that the mitochondrial enzyme is in fact this enzyme, since 
neither known form of DNA-ase has a pH optimum close to 6.0, and since 
the DNA which can be extracted from "non-gellable nuclei" shows by its fi- 
brous nature and stream birefringence (25) that it still possesses a high molecular 
weight The DNA-ase of high pH optimum, moreover, tends to be inactivated 
by citrate, if we may use pancreatic DNA-ase as an example; but liver nuclei 
isolated in very dilute citric acid at pH 6.0 suffer from the effect of the mito- 
chrondrial enzyme 
The mitochondrial enzyme might however be a protease, since proteases 
are known to occur in mitochondria (26) Indeed, DNA can be liberated from

work obviously will be required to determine the nature and action of the 
enzyme 
In the experiments with lysed mitochondria which demonstrate the presence 
of the "degelling" enzyme, we have not used unreasonable amounts or con- 
centrations of the mitochondrial extract The ratio of mitochondria from which 
the lysed extract was obtained to nuclei was about one to one, on a dry weight 
basis, whereas in the homogenate the corresponding ratio can be calculated 
as about 2.5 to 1 (assuming 25 per cent mitochondria and 10 per cent nuclei 
in the cells) It is true that the concentrations of both the soluble mitochondrial 
material and of the nuclei were 2 to 2.5 times greater in the incubation mixture 
than in the usual homogenate, but this concentration difference does not 
seem to be of sufficient magnitude to cast doubt upon the action of the mito- 
chondrial enzyme on nuclei during isolation procedures in which mitochondria 
are disrupted 
When nuclei are isolated at pH 6.0 in very dilute citric acid, practically all 
the mitochondria originally present in the homogenate are disrupted, so that 
a very large proportion of the mitochondrial enzyme must gain access to the 
nuclei These nuclei show no gel formation whatsoever When nuclei are 
isolated by methods involving less drastic homogenization, it is sometimes 
possible to obtain a product that shows some tendency to form gels but that 
does not show strong gel formation See footnote in Table I of the accompany- 
ing article (27)

mitochondria.protocol:
1. An improved type of ground glass homogenizer for soft tissues has been described which brings about a high degree of cell disruption and liberation of nuclei without causing appreciable damage to mitochondria.
2. A new method is described for the isolation of morphologically intact mitochondria and cell nuclei from the same homogenate. In this procedure, the pH of the homogenate in 0.44 M sucrose is maintained at 6.0-6.2 with citric acid during the homogenization.
3. The ot-ketoglutarate, malate, succinate, and hexanoate oxidases of the "intact" mitochondria isolated in 0.44 M sucrose adjusted to pH 6.0-6.2 with very dilute citric acid as described in this paper have been investigated, and it has been shown that the mitochondria compare favorably to those isolated in 0.25 M sucrose by a previously described method.
4. Mitochondria have been found to contain an enzyme which causes nuclei to lose their ability to form gels in dilute alkali.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides information on an improved ground glass homogenizer for cell disruption and liberation of nuclei without damaging mitochondria. It also describes a new method for isolating morphologically intact mitochondria and cell nuclei from the same homogenate. The article mentions the investigation of oxidases in the isolated mitochondria and the presence of an enzyme in mitochondria that prevents nuclei from forming gels in dilute alkali. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation, as it does not mention specific steps or details of the isolation process.

summary: Mitochondria prepared in 0.25 M sucrose at pH 6.2 with citrate swell partially. The enzyme responsible for preventing gel formation in dilute alkali is not yet known. It could be a nucleic acid or protein degrading enzyme, or a more specific enzyme that attacks the linkages between DNA and protein in the nucleus. The mitochondrial enzyme is unlikely to be DNA-ase due to differences in pH optimum and inactivation by citrate. It could potentially be a protease, as proteases are known to occur in mitochondria. Further research is needed to determine the nature and action of the enzyme. The concentration difference between the soluble mitochondrial material and the nuclei does not cast doubt on the action of the mitochondrial enzyme on nuclei during isolation procedures. Drastic homogenization disrupts most mitochondria, allowing a large proportion of the mitochondrial enzyme to access the nuclei. Nuclei isolated using less drastic homogenization may show some tendency to form gels, but not strong gel formation. An improved ground glass homogenizer has been developed for soft tissues, which effectively isolates nuclei without damaging mitochondria. A new method is described for isolating intact mitochondria and nuclei from the same homogenate. The pH of the homogenate is maintained at 6.0-6.2 with citric acid during homogenization. Mitochondria isolated using this method compare favorably to those isolated using a previously described method. Mitochondria contain an enzyme that prevents nuclei from forming gels in dilute alkali.

topics.topic: Unlikelihood of the enzyme being DNA-ase and potential involvement of protease
topics.justification: The summary states that the mitochondrial enzyme is unlikely to be DNA-ase due to differences in pH optimum and inactivation by citrate. topics.topic: Impact of homogenization on mitochondria and nuclei isolation
topics.justification: The summary discusses the impact of homogenization on mitochondria and nuclei isolation. It mentions that drastic homogenization disrupts most mitochondria, allowing the mitochondrial enzyme to access the nuclei. This topic explores the relationship between homogenization and the action of the mitochondrial enzyme on nuclei during isolation procedures.

topics.topic: Development of improved ground glass homogenizer for soft tissues
topics.justification: The summary mentions the development of an improved ground glass homogenizer for soft tissues, which effectively isolates nuclei without damaging mitochondria. This topic highlights the development of a new method for isolating intact mitochondria and nuclei from the same homogenate.

topics.topic: Comparison between mitochondria isolated using the new method and a previously described method
topics.justification: The summary mentions that mitochondria isolated using the new method compare favorably to those isolated using a previously described method. This topic focuses on the comparison between the two isolation methods and their effectiveness in isolating mitochondria.

topics.topic: Presence of an enzyme in mitochondria preventing nuclei from forming gels in dilute alkali
topics.justification: The summary concludes by stating that mitochondria contain an enzyme that prevents nuclei from forming gels in dilute alkali. This topic highlights the presence of the enzyme in mitochondria and its role in preventing gel formation in dilute alkali.
